title: Fibre blending for colour effect
subtitle: Using hand cards to blend differently coloured fibres into heathered, marled, and gradient handspun yarn
excerpt: Blending two or more colours of fibre on hand cards before spinning creates heathered, tweedy, or gradient effects that cannot be replicated by dyeing alone. This entry covers loading the cards for different blend ratios and reading the resulting colour before committing to a full batch.
sourceNotes: Fibre blending technique synthesised from general hand-carding and colour-blending practice. Colour proportions and the physics of visual mixing follow standard textile colour theory.

glossaryTerms.definition: Yarn with a speckled, mixed-colour appearance produced by blending differently coloured fibres before spinning. The eye mixes the colours optically, producing a tone different from either fibre alone.
The cylinder of carded fibre rolled off the hand card, ready for spinning. In colour blending, each rolag holds the blended fibres in a consistent proportion.

The proportion of each colour fibre loaded onto the card per rolag. A 50:50 ratio produces an even blend; a 70:30 ratio produces a yarn that reads predominantly as the majority colour with flecks of the minority.
A spinning method that drafts fibres loosely from a carded rolag, creating a soft, lofty yarn. Woollen draw is the natural companion to hand-carded blended preparations.

content.text: Blending coloured fibres on hand cards produces
heathered yarn

that cannot be achieved by dyeing. When two colours are carded together, the individual fibres remain distinct at close range but the eye mixes them at normal viewing distance, producing an optical blend. The perceived colour is controlled by the
colour ratio
of fibre loaded per
rolag
: changing the proportion by even 10% shifts the perceived colour noticeably. Plan the blend on a small test sample before carding a full batch.
Choosing colours

Analogous colours (neighbours on the colour wheel, like blue and green) blend into a clear intermediate tone. Complementary colours (opposites, like red and green) blend into a brown-grey because spinning spreads the fibres too finely for a vibrant mix. For a vibrant heather, blend within one colour family or keep the proportions strongly unequal (90:10) so one colour leads.
Method
Step 1: Weigh and separate the fibres

Decide on a colour ratio and weigh out the fibres accordingly. For a first blend, try 70% main colour and 30% accent colour. Divide each colour into small equal portions, one portion per rolag. Consistent portioning across the batch ensures consistent colour throughout the spun yarn. If you run out of one colour partway through, the yarn will shift perceptibly at that point.
Step 2: Load the card in layers

Load the main colour onto one card in thin, even passes. Add the accent colour on top in the same direction. Card 3-4 times, transferring the fibre between the two cards. The more passes, the more completely the colours blend and the more even the optical mix. For a tweedy effect with visible flecks, card only 2 times. Roll off the rolag and set aside. Repeat with each pair of portions to produce a uniform batch.
Step 3: Spin a test length
Spin a single rolag using the
woollen draw

and wind off a short skein of approximately 1 m. Wet the skein briefly and hang to dry. The twist setting and wetting will show the true blended colour more accurately than the dry singles. Compare against your planned colour. If the blend reads too light, increase the accent colour proportion in the next batch; if too dark, reduce it.
Step 4: Spin the full batch

Spin through all the rolags at a consistent twist ratio to produce a uniform colour across the full batch. Keep rolags in the order they were carded: any slight variation in loading is distributed evenly rather than concentrated in one section of the yarn. Ply and finish the yarn as for any woollen-spun singles.

items.problem: The colour of the spun yarn is much duller than the individual fibres suggested
items.cause: The two colours cancel each other out because they are complementary colours mixed in close to equal parts
items.fix: Use a strongly unequal ratio like 80:20 so one colour leads. Or switch to analogous colours (neighbours on the wheel), which blend into a clear tone rather than a grey.

items.problem: The colour is uneven along the length of the yarn, appearing in patches
items.cause: The fibre portions were inconsistent in weight, producing rolags with different colour ratios
items.fix: Weigh each colour portion on a kitchen scale before loading each card. Consistency in the weight of fibre loaded per card is the main variable in batch colour.

items.problem: The accent colour disappears completely in the finished yarn
items.cause: The accent proportion was too small or the fibres were carded too many times, blending the colours into a uniform tone
items.fix: Increase the accent proportion to at least 20%, or reduce to 2-3 card passes to retain visible flecks of the accent colour in the finished yarn.
